steps.ttsScript: Words rhyme when they have the same ending sound. Let me show you!

steps.ttsScript: The middle is the biggest part of the story. It is everything that happens after the beginning, before the very end.
Say a puppy got lost at the start. In the middle, the puppy looks and looks for home. The middle holds the action! Always tell the middle, not just the beginning and end.
Here's a little story. Listen carefully!
steps.interaction: Heading appears. Storybook opens on screen.

steps.ttsScript: And last? The babies hatched! You just retold a whole story! Amazing!

steps.ttsScript: Let's break it down! Characters can be people or animals in a story.
The setting is the place and time. Like a school, a beach, or a forest!
Events are the things that happen in the story. Like a race, a party, or an adventure!
So remember: who, where, and what happened. Those are the three parts!

steps.ttsScript: When we read a fact book, it's usually all about one big thing. That big thing is called the main topic!
Like a book about dogs — the main topic is dogs! Everything in it tells us about dogs.
Let's learn how to find the main topic in any fact book!

steps.ttsScript: The main topic is what the whole book is mostly about.

steps.ttsScript: In fact books, two pieces of information can go together, like puzzle pieces that fit!
Making a connection means finding how two facts are about the same thing.
Let's learn to link facts together!
Two facts connect when they belong to the same thing.
Like this: A frog has long back legs. A frog can jump very far. Both facts are about the frog, so they link together!
Linking two facts helps us understand the whole picture!
Listen to two facts about a bird. Let's find how they link!
Fact one: An owl has big round eyes.
steps.displayText: An owl has big round eyes.

steps.ttsScript: Fact two: An owl hunts at night.
How do they link? Both facts are about the owl! Big eyes and hunting at night go together.

The long sound says its name! A! Cake!

steps.ttsScript: If both answers are yes, your sentence is perfect!

steps.ttsScript: Sometimes we hear or read a word we don't know. That's totally normal!
The great news is there are tricks to figure out what new words mean!
Let's learn how to crack the code!

steps.ttsScript: When you hear a word you don't know, listen to the other words around it.
The words before and after give you clues about what the new word means.
You can also ask a grown-up, or look at pictures for help!
Context clues plus pictures plus asking — that's the triple combo!

steps.ttsScript: Did you know that words can be related to each other? Like friends and family!
Some words are opposites — like 'hot' and 'cold.' Some words are similar — like 'happy' and 'glad!'
Let's explore how words are connected!

steps.ttsScript: Opposites are words that mean the complete reverse. Big and small. Hot and cold. Up and down!
They're like two sides of a coin!
Words can also belong to groups. 'Dog,' 'cat,' and 'fish' are all animals!
And some words mean almost the same thing! 'Big' and 'large' are alike!

steps.ttsScript: What's the opposite of 'happy'?

steps.ttsScript: You learn new words all the time! But the best part is using them!
When you put a new word into your own sentence, that word becomes yours!
Let's practice using new words in sentences!
When you learn a new word, build a sentence around it right away!
Say you learn the word 'gigantic.' You could say: The blue whale is gigantic!
See how the word fit right into the sentence? That is using a new word!
Let's try one together! Here's a new word: 'cozy.' It means warm and snug.
Now let's use it in a sentence. Which one sounds right? My bed is cozy!
You just used 'cozy' in your very own sentence! You're a vocabulary superstar!

slides.heading: Let's Use a New Word!
slides.imagePrompt: A happy young child snuggled cozily in bed under a thick warm blanket, hugging a soft pillow, in a softly lit bedroom — conveying warm, snug, and cozy.

steps.ttsScript: Here's a trick. To use a new word, ask: what could I say about it?
Then start your sentence and slide the new word right inside!
One little sentence, and that word is yours forever!
Now you try! Here's a brand-new word: 'joyful.' It means very happy. Which sentence uses the word joyful the right way?

interactive.hint: Joyful means very happy. Find the sentence where it fits!
choices: We were joyful at the party!
I drank a glass of joyful.
interactive.correct: We were joyful at the party!
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Joyful means very happy. Find the sentence where it fits just right. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! 'We were joyful at the party!' You used the new word in a sentence all by yourself!

steps.ttsScript: You're putting new words into your very own sentences!
Let's practice using new words the right way. Let's go!
Great readers are like detectives. They look for clues in a story.
They ask questions and hunt for answers in the words.
Let us learn some detective questions to ask!
Story detectives ask Who, What, and Where.

steps.ttsScript: Good detectives ask five big questions.
Who is the story about?
What are they doing?
Where are they? And when?
And why did it happen?

steps.ttsScript: Retelling means telling a story in your own words.
You keep the most important parts.
And you tell them in order.
Every story has three big parts, in order.
First, the beginning. Who is in it and what is happening?
Next, the middle. What is the problem or big event?
Last, the end. How does it all work out?
Let's try one together. Listen to this story.
Tom wanted to win the race. His shoe came untied! He tied it fast and crossed the finish line.
steps.displayText: Tom wanted to win the race. His shoe came untied! He tied it and finished.

steps.ttsScript: What happened at the beginning? Tom wanted to win!
What happened in the middle? His shoe came untied!
And the end? He tied it and finished! You retold all three parts!
Here is a trick. Use order words.
Say first, then next, then last.
Now you are ready to practice retelling!

Now you retell! Here's a new story. Lily wanted to bake muffins. She found her bowl was empty with no flour! So she borrowed some from her neighbor and baked them. Match each event to its part of the story.

interactive.hint: First the beginning, then the middle problem, then the end.
interactive.prompt: Match each event to its part
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Think about what happened first, next, and last. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You retold the whole story in order! Beginning, she wanted muffins. Middle, no flour. End, she borrowed some and baked!

steps.ttsScript: Every story is built from three big pieces.
Characters, setting, and events. The who, where, and what.
Let us look at each one.

steps.ttsScript: Writers pick special words to make us feel something.
Some words let us hear, see, or touch a story in our mind.
Let us go find some feeling words!
Today we learn words that tell us how a character feels.
Words like happy, excited, or joyful show us good feelings.
Words like sad, angry, or scared show us harder feelings.
These feeling words help us understand the story.
Watch how one word changes everything. Here is a plain sentence: She walked to the door.
Now I swap in a feeling word. She stomped to the door. Stomped makes me feel she is angry! Same girl, but one word shows how she feels inside.
Let's try one together. Listen to this sentence.
The puppy bounced across the yard.
steps.displayText: The puppy bounced across the yard.

steps.ttsScript: Which word shows the puppy feels happy? Bounced!
Bounced makes us feel how excited and happy that puppy is!
Here is a helpful trick.
When you read a feeling word, stop and feel it for a moment.
Now you are ready to find feeling words on your own!
Your turn! Read this sentence. The hungry boy gobbled his lunch. Which word shows how the boy feels?

interactive.hint: Which word makes you feel it? Listen for the doing word.
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Which word shows how he ate? Listen again and try once more!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Gobbled! That word lets us feel just how hungry the boy was!

steps.ttsScript: You are a feeling-word finder now! Let's practice.

steps.ttsScript: Every story has a storyteller.

steps.ttsScript: If the story uses the word I, a character is telling it.
Listen. I went to the store with my mom.
steps.displayText: I went to the store with my mom.

steps.ttsScript: The person saying I is the one telling the story. We call that first person.

steps.ttsScript: If the story uses he, she, or they, a voice outside is telling it.
Listen. Sam ran to the park. He played on the swings.

steps.ttsScript: Every fact book is mostly about one big idea.
That one big idea is called the main topic.
The little facts that tell us more are called key details.
Listen. Apples can be red or green. They grow on trees. Apples are a healthy snack.
steps.displayText: Apples can be red or green. They grow on trees.

steps.ttsScript: What one word do we hear again and again? Apples. So the main topic is apples.
The key details tell us more about apples: their color, where they grow, and that they are healthy.
Let us try one together. Listen to this little book.
Firefighters wear special gear. They drive fire trucks. They help put out fires.
steps.displayText: Firefighters wear gear. They drive trucks. They put out fires.

steps.ttsScript: What is this book mostly about? Firefighters. That is the main topic.
Now the key details. What do firefighters wear? Special gear.
What do they drive? Fire trucks. And what do they do? Put out fires. Three key details, all about firefighters!
Here is a helpful trick. The word that comes up the most is usually the main topic.
The key details are the little facts that tell you more about that topic.
Your turn, detective! Listen closely. Bees live in a hive. They buzz from flower to flower. Bees make sweet honey. What is the main topic?

interactive.hint: Which word do you hear again and again?

interactive.correctScript: Yes! Bees! That word came up the most, so it is the main topic. The honey and flowers are just key details!

steps.ttsScript: You found the main topic and the key details! Now it is time to practice on your own.

steps.ttsScript: Fact books have special parts that help you find facts fast.
Today we meet three helpers: contents, headings, and labels.
Each one helps you find something different. Let us see how!
The table of contents is up front. It lists chapters and their page numbers.
A heading is the big words at the top. It tells what the page is about.
A label is a little word that points to a part of a picture.
Let's try one together! You want to find facts about frogs.
Which part shows you what page frogs start on? The contents!

displayParts.text: What page do frogs start on?

steps.ttsScript: Now you are on the frog page. Which part names the frog's legs? The label!

steps.ttsScript: Listen! Every word is made of little sounds, hiding one right after another.
When we push those sounds together into one word, we call it blending.
Use your ears for this one. Let's blend some sounds into real words!
Listen to three sounds, one at a time. Mmm. Aaa. Nnn.
Now push them together fast. Mmm-aaa-nnn. Man! That is blending.
Let's blend one together. Close your eyes and just listen.
Here are the three sounds. Mmm. Ooo. P.
How many sounds did you hear? Three sounds!
Now push them together fast. Mmm-ooo-p. Mop!
You blended mmm, ooo, p into mop. Blending power!
Here's the trick. Say each sound slowly. Sss-iii-t.
Then say them faster and faster until they snap together. Sit!
Now you blend! Listen to these three sounds. Sss. Uuu. Nnn. Push them together fast. What word do they make?

interactive.hint: Say the sounds slowly, then snap them together fast.
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! Say the sounds again, slow then fast. Sss-uuu-nnn. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Sss-uuu-nnn blends into sun! Your ears blended it all by yourself!

steps.ttsScript: Great readers and writers pick the just-right word.
The right word makes a sentence clear!
Let us practice.

steps.ttsScript: A banquet? Yes! A symphony? No!
Let's try one together. Here is a sentence with a blank.
The storm was loud. The thunder was very blank.
Which word fits? Noisy? Yes!
Why not silent? Silent means no sound at all!
So the thunder was very noisy. You picked the just-right word!
Here is a helpful trick.
Ask, does this word fit the meaning of my sentence?
Now you are ready to use big words!

interactive.wrongScript: So close! The puppy was small. Which word means small? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Tiny! Tiny means very small, so it fits the puppy perfectly!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Let's practice picking the just-right word.

Every story has a shape. A beginning, middle, and end.
The shape helps us follow along.
Let us look at each part.
The beginning introduces us. Who is the story about? Where are they?
It sets up the whole story.
The middle is where most of the action happens.
There is usually a problem or a big event.
The end wraps things up. How did it all turn out?
Let us map a real story together. Here it is.
Sam woke up on the farm. His goat got loose and ran off! Sam chased it and brought it home.
steps.displayText: Sam's goat got loose. Sam brought it home.

steps.ttsScript: How does it start? Sam wakes up on the farm. That is the beginning!
What is the problem? The goat got loose! That is the middle.
How does it finish? Sam brings the goat home. That is the end. You mapped the whole shape!

steps.ttsScript: This year your stories get longer and trickier. Sometimes a part feels confusing.
Good readers do not just keep going. They use a trick called rereading.
To reread means to read a tricky part again, slowly, to understand it.
When a part feels confusing, stop. Do not rush past it.
Read that part again, and ask yourself: what is happening here?
Let's try one together. Here is a tricky little part of a story.
Sam froze. The shadow on the wall was huge. Then his dog wagged its tail.
steps.displayText: Sam froze. The shadow was huge. Then his dog wagged its tail.

steps.ttsScript: Wait, that was confusing. What made the shadow? Let's stop and reread.
Reading again, the dog wagged its tail. So the dog made the big shadow!
Rereading helped us understand. Now the tricky part makes sense!
Here's the trick: if your brain says huh, back up and read it again.
Now you try! Here's a tricky part. Mia heard a tap, tap, tap. She turned around. A branch was knocking on her window. What was making the tapping sound?

interactive.hint: Reread the story. What knocked on the window?

steps.ttsScript: Now it's time to practice rereading tricky parts on your own. Let's go!
Smart readers ask questions as they read facts.

Every one has an answer in the text.

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Listen: When the icy wind blows, the penguins huddle close together in a big group. Why do the penguins huddle together?

steps.ttsScript: Longer fact texts have lots of little ideas.
But one big idea holds them all together.
That big idea is called the main topic.
Each paragraph focuses on a smaller part of the big idea.
One detail tells what something is.
Another detail tells why it matters.
All the details point back to the main topic.
Let's try one together. Here is a short text about honey bees.
Bees buzz from flower to flower. They turn nectar into sweet honey.
steps.displayText: Bees gather nectar and make honey.

steps.ttsScript: Is the big idea buzzing, or honey bees?
Honey bees! Buzzing is just one little detail. Every sentence is about the bees.
Here is a helpful trick.
Don't just pick the word you hear most. Ask: what is this text mostly about?
The big idea covers every sentence, not just one.

Your turn, detective! Listen closely. 'Penguins waddle across the ice on their short legs. They dive into the cold sea to catch fish. Their thick feathers keep them warm in the freezing cold.' Which sentence tells the BIG idea of the whole text?

interactive.hint: One sentence covers the whole text. The others are just single details.
choices: Penguins are built for life in the icy cold.
Penguins dive into the sea to catch fish.
Penguins have short legs for waddling.
interactive.correct: Penguins are built for life in the icy cold.
interactive.wrongScript: That's a real detail, but it's only one sentence. Which choice covers the WHOLE text? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! That's the big idea! The legs, the diving, and the warm feathers all connect to living in the icy cold.

steps.ttsScript: You're ready! Time to practice finding the big idea on your own.
Remember, the big idea ties every sentence together.
Fact books are full of real events. And those events connect to each other!
One thing makes the next thing happen. That is a connection.
Let us learn to connect real events in history and science!
A cause is the reason something happens.
An effect is what happens because of it. The cause leads to the effect.
The word because shows a connection. It links the cause to the effect.
Like this. The pond froze because the air got cold.
Let us try one together. Here is a fact about the sun and rain.
The sun heats the water in a lake. The warm water rises up and makes clouds.
steps.displayText: Sun heats water. Water rises to make clouds.

steps.ttsScript: What is the cause? The sun heats the water!
What is the effect? The water rises and makes clouds!
So clouds form because the sun heats the water. You connected two real ideas!
steps.displayText: Clouds form because of the sun

steps.ttsScript: Here is a trick. When you read a fact, ask why did this happen?
The answer is the cause. Now you can connect any real events!

interactive.prompt: Match the cause to its effect
interactive.wrongScript: Good try! Ask why does each one happen? Match the cause to its effect. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! Rain makes plants grow, the setting sun makes the sky dark, and planted seeds make flowers bloom. You connected real events!

steps.ttsScript: You are a connection detective now! Time to practice connecting real events.

title: How Authors Back Up Their Points

steps.ttsScript: When an author makes a point, they give reasons.
The reasons back up the point.
Spotting reasons makes you a strong reader!
Point: you should drink water every day.
Why? Reason one: it helps your body work.
Reason two: it stops you from feeling tired.
Two reasons hold up the point!
Let's try one together. Here's an author's point.
steps.displayText: Recess is the best part of school.

steps.ttsScript: The author says we run, we play, and we make friends.
Which reason backs up the point? We make friends!
That reason backs up the point. You found it!
Here is a helpful trick.
Watch for words like because, since, and for example. Those signal reasons!
After those words comes a reason!

slides.heading: Look for Because
slides.imagePrompt: A friendly cartoon magnifying glass with a face hovering over an open book page, beams of light highlighting a glowing blank underlined gap on the page where a key word would sit; no letters or words anywhere. Bright 2D cartoon, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors.

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen closely. 'Dogs make great pets. They keep you safe because they bark at strangers. They also play fetch and love long walks.' Which reason backs up the point that dogs make great pets?

interactive.hint: Listen after 'because' — find a reason the author gives.
interactive.prompt: Which reason backs up the point?
interactive.wrongScript: That's the point, not a reason. Listen again — what reason does the author give? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! They bark at strangers is a reason that backs up the point! You spotted the proof!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Now you'll find the reasons authors give.
Remember to listen for because and since.
Two fact books can be about the very same topic.
But each author picks different details to share.
Reading both gives you a bigger picture.
First, find what both books agree on. That is the same.
Then, find what only one book says. That is different.
Comparing means spotting both at once!
Let's try one together. Two books are about frogs.
Book One says: Frogs hop. They eat bugs. Book Two says: Frogs hop. They lay eggs in water.
steps.displayText: Book 1: hop, eat bugs. Book 2: hop, lay eggs.

steps.ttsScript: What do both books say? Frogs hop!
What is only in Book Two? They lay eggs!
You found the same point and the different one. Great comparing!

steps.ttsScript: Some words have a letter you write but never say.
We call it a silent letter. It hides in the word.
Let us learn how to find them.
In knee, the K is silent. We just say nee.
In lamb, the B is silent. We just say lam.
In wrist, the W is silent. We just say rist.
Silent letters love to hide at the start of a word.
Like the silent K in knot and the silent W in wrap.
They also hide at the end, like the silent B in climb.

steps.ttsScript: Stories and poems are built from parts, like chapters and stanzas.
And each part builds on the one before it.
steps.displayText: Each part builds on the last

steps.ttsScript: A stanza is a group of lines in a poem, like a paragraph.
The first stanza sets things up. The next one moves the idea forward.
Let's try one together. Here is a short poem with two stanzas.
First stanza: The seed slept deep below the ground, waiting and warm, not making a sound.
steps.displayText: The seed slept deep below the ground, waiting and warm, not making a sound.

steps.ttsScript: What happens in stanza one? The seed waits underground!
Second stanza: Then up popped a leaf in the morning light, stretching up tall to the warm and bright.
steps.displayText: Then up popped a leaf in the morning light, stretching up tall.

steps.ttsScript: How does stanza two build on stanza one? The seed grows into a plant! Part two moves the story forward.

steps.displayText: Each part builds on the last

steps.ttsScript: Now you try! Here is a poem. Stanza one: A storm rolled in, the sky turned gray, rain poured down all through the day. Stanza two: But morning came with golden sun, and out we ran to play and run. How does stanza two build on stanza one?

interactive.correctScript: Yes! Stanza two builds on stanza one by showing the storm pass and the sun arrive. You followed how the parts connect!

steps.ttsScript: Did you ever read two different stories that taught the same big lesson?
steps.displayText: Two stories can share an idea

steps.ttsScript: The big idea or lesson in a story is called the theme.
When we compare two stories, we look at how their themes and plots are alike and different.
Two stories can have very different characters and places but still teach the same theme.
A story about a turtle and a story about a runner can both teach: never give up.
To compare two stories, ask what each one teaches, then find the idea they share.
The plots can be totally different even when the theme is exactly the same.
Let's try one together. Here are two short stories.
Story one: Ant carried a crumb up the hill. It slipped down again and again, but Ant kept climbing until it reached the top.
steps.displayText: Ant kept climbing until it reached the top.

steps.ttsScript: Story two: Kira missed the basket many times. She practiced every day until she finally made the shot.
steps.displayText: Kira practiced until she made the shot.

steps.ttsScript: What does each story teach? Both characters keep trying after they fail.

steps.ttsScript: Events and ideas in nonfiction often connect.
Cause and effect. Sequence. Compare and contrast.
One thing makes another happen. The water spilled because the glass tipped.
Look for signal words like because, so, and as a result.
A cause makes an effect. The why and the what.
Sequence shows order. Compare and contrast shows same and different.
Let's try one together. Here's a true event.
The plant wilted because nobody watered it for a week.
steps.displayText: The plant wilted because nobody watered it.

steps.ttsScript: What signal word connects them? Because!
What caused the wilting? No water.
No water caused the plant to wilt. You connected cause and effect!

interactive.correctScript: Yes! The icy sidewalk caused Sam to slip. The word 'so' connected the cause and the effect!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn to connect events and ideas on your own!

steps.ttsScript: A suffix is an ending we add to a word.
The ending gives us a clue to the word's meaning.
The suffix T-I-O-N turns an action into a thing.
Add it to act, and you get action. Add it to invent, and you get invention.
The suffix A-B-L-E means able to be done.
Break a word into its base and its ending. Wash plus able is washable.
Let's try decoding a word together. Here it is.
What is the base word? Break!
What is the suffix? Able!
So breakable means able to be broken. You decoded it!

steps.ttsScript: Some words have a letter you write but never say out loud.
We call it a silent letter. Today we hunt for them!
Look at the word knee. We do not say the K. We just say nee.
The K is written, but it stays silent. Your mouth skips right over it.
In the word comb, the B is silent. We say kome, not komb.
To find a silent letter, say the word slowly and listen for a letter you do not hear.
Let's try one together. Here's a word.
Here is the word: write.
Say it slowly. We hear rite. What letter do we not hear?
The W! The W at the front is silent.
So write starts with a silent W. You found the quiet letter!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen to this story. Lily the mouse saw a pumpkin in the garden. It was enormous! Lily had never seen such a big pumpkin before. Now, what does enormous mean?

steps.ttsScript: Hello, word detectives! Are you ready to uncover some secrets today?
Hard words often have clues right beside them.
These clues help us understand what difficult words mean, making reading easier!

steps.ttsScript: Prefixes change a word's meaning right at the start.
Take happy. Add un at the front. Now it's unhappy — meaning not happy!
Let's explore some common prefixes and see the power they have!

slides.heading: Front-of-the-Word Power
slides.imagePrompt: A friendly cartoon kid holding a small glowing puzzle piece in their hand, about to click it onto the LEFT SIDE of a large blank colorful shape. Side-view composition, the kid on the left, the shape on the right. Soft sunlit pastel background. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors. No text, no words, no letters anywhere in the image. Natural-looking eyes, no twinkles, no sparkles, no AI-generated glints. No text overlays, no hallucinated words, no signs with writing.

steps.ttsScript: Unhappy. The un at the start means not. Unhappy means not happy.
Redo. The re at the start means again. Redo means do again.
Preview. The pre at the start means before. Preview means see before.

steps.ttsScript: Disagree. The dis at the start means opposite. Disagree means not agree.
Misspell. The mis at the start means wrongly. Misspell means spell wrong.
Sub at the start means under. Submarine means under water.

steps.ttsScript: A dictionary tells you what a word means.
It also shows you how to say it.
Let's learn to look one up.
Words sit in alphabetical order.
Two guide words sit at the top of each page.
Your word fits between them.
Let's look up a word together. Here is the entry.
Here is the entry: gobble, gob-uhl, to eat something fast.
How do we say it? Gob-uhl!
What does it mean? To eat fast!
You read the whole entry. Nice work!

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
An entry always goes word, then sound, then meaning.
Read it in that order every time!

interactive.hint: Read the last part of the entry — the meaning.
interactive.wrongScript: Not yet! Look at the last part of the entry — what does it say it means? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Easy to break! You read the meaning right from the entry!

steps.ttsScript: Some words mean more than what they say.
Figurative language paints a picture or feeling.
It helps us understand things in a new and exciting way!

steps.ttsScript: Simile uses like or as. He ran like a cheetah.
Metaphor says something is something else. Her smile is sunshine.

steps.ttsScript: Idioms have hidden meanings. Piece of cake means easy!

steps.ttsScript: Let's try one together. Here is a sentence.
The brave knight was as quiet as a mouse.
steps.displayText: The brave knight was as quiet as a mouse.

steps.ttsScript: What type of figurative language is this? A simile! It uses the word as to compare.
So what does it really mean? Very quiet! A mouse is super quiet, so the knight was too.
It is a simile that means very quiet. You found the hidden meaning!

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
When words sound weird literally, look for a hidden meaning!

steps.ttsScript: You've learned about different kinds of figurative language. Now, your turn to match each type to what it does!

interactive.hint: Think about what each type does.
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite! Think about the special words or ideas for each type. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Exactly! Similes use 'like' or 'as', metaphors say something 'is' something else, and idioms have hidden meanings. Great job!

steps.ttsScript: Some phrases mean exactly what they say. Others paint a picture in your mind!
Literal means the exact, real meaning of the words.
Nonliteral means the words paint a picture and mean something different.
Listen to this phrase. The classroom was a zoo this morning.
Were there really lions and monkeys in the room? No! So it is not literal.
It is nonliteral. It really means the room was wild and noisy.
Let's try one together. Here's a phrase.
It was raining cats and dogs all afternoon.
Are cats and dogs really falling from the sky? No! So this is nonliteral.
What does it really mean? It is raining very hard!
The words sound silly literally, so the meaning is nonliteral. You pictured it!
Here is a helpful trick. If the words sound silly when you picture them literally, the meaning is nonliteral!
Your turn! Sam said the math test was a piece of cake. He did not really eat cake! What does a piece of cake really mean?

interactive.hint: Cake is sweet and simple. Is the test hard or easy?
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite! Picture it — would something easy or hard be called a piece of cake? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! A piece of cake means very easy! You found the nonliteral meaning all by yourself!

steps.ttsScript: Now you can spot literal and nonliteral meanings. Time to practice!
Some words describe real moments in life.
Cozy means warm and snug, like a blanket fort.
Brisk means quick and lively, like a fast walk.
To really know a word, picture a real moment that fits it.
Reading under a warm blanket feels cozy.
Let's try one together. Here is a real moment.
We ran fast in the cool, windy air all morning.
Which word fits this moment, cozy or brisk?
Brisk! Running fast in cool wind feels quick and lively. You matched the word to the moment!
Ask yourself: when would I really use this word?
Your turn! Here is a moment. We curled up by the fire with warm cocoa. Which word fits this moment best?

interactive.hint: Warm and snug by the fire is which word?
interactive.wrongScript: Close! Think how warm and snug by a fire feels. Which word is that? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Cozy! Curled up warm by the fire is exactly a cozy moment. You matched the word to real life!

steps.ttsScript: Time to practice matching words to real moments!
Have you ever noticed that some words have similar meanings?
Some words have similar meanings but different strengths.
Today, we'll explore these different levels of word strength!

Which one is the strongest? Freezing!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! We learned that some words have different levels of strength. Which word means extremely hungry?

interactive.hint: Think about which word shows the highest level of hunger.
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Remember, some words show a little hunger, and some show a lot. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Exactly! Famished means extremely hungry. It's the strongest word for hunger!

steps.ttsScript: Some words are like little signals. They tell your reader when something happens, or where it happens.
Time words tell when. Place words tell where. Let's learn to use them.
Time words tell when something happens. Words like before, after, and during.
Place words tell where something is. Words like here, there, and behind.
Let's try one together. Here is a sentence missing a word.
We packed our bags. Blank, we drove to the lake.
Does this signal a time or a place? It tells when, so we need a time word!
After we packed, we drove. After tells when!
After is a time word. It tells your reader when. Nicely done!
Here is the trick. Ask yourself: does this word tell when, or where?
Your turn! Match each signal word to what it tells us, time or place.

interactive.hint: Ask yourself: does this word tell you WHEN, or WHERE?
interactive.prompt: Match each word to what it tells
interactive.wrongScript: Good try! Ask yourself: does the word tell when, or where? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! After and before tell time, and behind tells place!

steps.ttsScript: Time to practice! Let's use signal words on your own.
Fourth grade readers use specific details from the text as evidence.
Sometimes the answer is right there. Sometimes you have to figure it out.
So, remember: good details prove your answer!
When you make a claim, point to the exact words from the text.
Other times the answer is hidden. You must infer it by putting clues together.
Both ways, the details are your proof.
Let's try one together. Here's a little story.
Sam tiptoed past the cake, then hid the gifts behind the couch and turned off all the lights.
steps.displayText: Sam hid the gifts behind the couch and turned off the lights.

steps.ttsScript: Where did Sam hide the gifts? Behind the couch! The text says it.
Why turn off the lights? It's a surprise party! We infer that from the clues.
One answer was stated, one we inferred. Nice detective work!
Here is a helpful trick.
When you answer, say: the words tell me... and name your clue!
Now you are ready to find story details!

Your turn, detective! Listen carefully to this short story. Lily pulled on her thickest sweater and wrapped a long scarf around her neck. She grabbed her mittens before heading out the door. What is the weather like outside?

interactive.hint: What clothes did Lily grab? Picture the weather that needs them.
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Think about Lily's sweater, scarf, and mittens. What weather needs those? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Cold! The sweater, scarf, and mittens were your clues. Great inferring!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen closely to this story. Pip the little bird wanted to fly. He flapped his wings and fell. He tried again and wobbled. He tried one more time, pushed hard, and soared high above the trees! What's the big lesson this story shares?

steps.ttsScript: Authors show traits through action, not just labels.
He helped the lost child means kind,
even without saying kind.

steps.ttsScript: Words like soared, drowning, or melting often mean something figurative.
Figurative language uses words in a way that is not literal.
It helps create a special effect or meaning in a story.

Maria was drowning in homework all weekend.
steps.displayText: Maria was drowning in homework all weekend.

steps.ttsScript: Is there real water here? No. This is not literal. There is no actual water.
So what does it mean? She had way too much homework, like it was burying her.
Drowning paints a feeling, not real water. You unlocked the figurative meaning!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, word detective! Listen: 'After the kind note, Sam felt his cold heart melting.' What does 'melting' mean here?

interactive.hint: He isn't really turning to liquid.
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Sam isn't really turning to liquid. Picture how his feelings change. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Softening up, because his feelings are changing, not his body.

steps.ttsScript: Stories come in three big forms.
These forms are poems,
drama, and prose.
Poems have lines, stanzas, and often rhythm or rhyme.
Drama is meant to be acted out. It uses scenes and dialogue.
Prose is regular sentences and paragraphs.
Let's try one together. Here is a short piece of writing.
The moon hangs high, a silver dime. It glows above the sea each night.
steps.displayText: The moon hangs high, a silver dime. / It glows above the sea each night.

steps.ttsScript: What clues do you see? It has short lines that rhyme!
So which form is it? A poem! Lines and rhyme give it away.

slides.heading: Let's Try One
slides.imagePrompt: A friendly K-4 diverse child with pigtails stands on a sandy beach at night, looking up with a thoughtful and appreciative expression. High in the dark sky, a large, bright, round silver moon glows, casting a gentle light over the calm, dark blue sea below. Solid dark blue background. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors. Absolutely no text, no letters, no words, no numbers, no labels anywhere. Natural proportions, no AI artifacts, no eye glints.

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
Each form has its own clue word: stanza, scene, or paragraph.
Find the clue, and you find the form!
Now you try! Read this part. Scene One. Max walks in and says, Hello! Then Mia answers him. Which form is this?

interactive.hint: Look for the word Scene and characters speaking lines.
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Look again. It says Scene One and the characters speak lines. Which form is acted out? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! It's drama! The word Scene and the characters speaking show it's meant to be acted out.

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Let's practice spotting poems, drama, and prose.
Every story is told by a narrator. The narrator decides whose eyes we see through.
Some narrators say 'I'. Others say 'he' or 'she'. And some can even read minds!
When the narrator says 'he' or 'she' but only knows ONE character's thoughts, that is third-person limited.
It is like following one person around. You hear only what that person feels.
When the narrator knows what EVERY character is thinking, that is third-person omniscient.
Omniscient means all-knowing. This narrator floats above and sees inside everyone.
Let's try one together. Here is a short passage.
Maya gripped her bat, scared she might miss. Across the field, Leo hoped the ball would fly his way.
steps.displayText: Maya felt scared. Leo hoped the ball came his way.

steps.ttsScript: Does the narrator say I? No! It says Maya and Leo. So is it first or third person? Third!
Now, whose thoughts do we hear? We feel Maya's fear AND Leo's hope. Two minds!
Two minds means all-knowing. So this is third-person omniscient. You did it!

slides.heading: Let's Try One
slides.imagePrompt: A cartoon girl with a baseball bat stands at home plate, gripping the bat, with a slightly nervous but determined expression. Across a green field, a cartoon boy in an outfield position looks up with a hopeful expression, hands ready to catch. A baseball is in the air, flying towards the boy. Solid sky-blue background. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors. Absolutely no text, no letters, no words, no numbers, no labels anywhere. Natural proportions, no AI artifacts, no eye glints.

steps.ttsScript: Here is a handy trick to remember.
Count the minds you can read. One mind is limited. Every mind is omniscient.
Now you try, detective! Listen closely. 'Sam stared at the test, sure he would fail. Beside him, Priya secretly worried she had studied the wrong page.' Whose thoughts does the narrator share?

interactive.hint: Count how many characters' thoughts you heard.
interactive.wrongScript: Think again. How many characters' thoughts did you hear in that passage? Try once more!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! You heard Sam's worry AND Priya's worry. Two minds means omniscient!

steps.ttsScript: You're ready! Time to practice spotting each narrator on your own.
A story can be read as words, or watched as a movie.
Today we line up a story's exact words with what we see on screen.
Authors give us exact details, like a green cloak with a silver clasp.
steps.displayText: Story details: green cloak, silver clasp

steps.ttsScript: A good movie shows those very things on screen, not just any cloak.
Let's try one. Here is a line from a story.
The old lighthouse glowed red as waves crashed against the rocks.
steps.displayText: Lighthouse glowed red, waves crashed on rocks.

steps.ttsScript: Which thing must the movie show? A red glowing light!
And one more? Waves crashing on the rocks!
Here is a handy trick to remember.
Point to the exact words, then find that very thing on screen.
Your turn, director! Here is a sentence: A small fox darted past the frozen pond as snow fell in big white flakes. To match these exact words, what must the movie show on screen?

interactive.hint: Find the things you could actually see, then picture them on screen.
choices: A fox by a frozen pond, snow falling
A bright sunny meadow in spring
How lonely the fox is feeling
interactive.correct: A fox by a frozen pond, snow falling
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Look back at the words a camera could really show. A fox, a frozen pond, falling snow. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! A fox by a frozen pond with snow falling. You matched the exact words to the screen!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Let's line up more story words with the screen.
Big ideas like courage, family, and home.
appear in stories all over the world.

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen to this tiny story. A clever rabbit wanted to eat the juicy carrots in Farmer McGregor's garden, but the farmer always watched. The rabbit pretended to be hurt, hopping slowly and limping past the farmer. When the farmer went to help, the rabbit quickly darted into the garden and ate all the carrots! What kind of story is this?

interactive.hint: Think about the main character.
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite! Remember, trickster tales often involve a clever character outsmarting someone. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Exactly! That's a trickster tale because the clever rabbit used a trick to get what it wanted.

steps.ttsScript: When a story gets longer, we read closely to understand it.
We watch the characters, the problem, and the small details.
Good readers ask who the story is about.
Then they ask what that person wants.
Watch for little signs
that show a hero's mood
and what they do next.
Let's try one together. Here's part of a longer story.
Maya stared at the dark cave. Her hands shook, but she wanted to find her lost dog.
steps.displayText: Maya stared at the dark cave. Her hands shook, but she wanted her lost dog.

steps.ttsScript: How does Maya feel? Scared. Her shaking hands are the sign.
What does Maya want? To find her dog. That is why she keeps going.
Scared, but brave! You read the signs and understood Maya.
Here is a helpful trick.
Make a movie in your mind as you read each scene!
steps.displayText: Make a movie in your mind

steps.ttsScript: Then the little signs become easy to spot!

Your turn, detective! Here's a new story. The judge read Lena's name, and the whole crowd cheered. She held up her science ribbon and couldn't stop grinning. What is Lena most likely feeling at the science fair?

interactive.hint: She held up her ribbon and couldn't stop grinning. What does that grin tell you?
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! She won a ribbon, held it up high, and couldn't stop grinning. How would that make her feel? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Proud! Holding up her ribbon and grinning after winning was the sign!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Now you'll read story bits on your own.
Follow each character and read the little signs.
Fourth grade nonfiction readers back up answers with text evidence.
Some answers are stated right in the words.
Other answers are hidden. You figure them out from clues.
Let's try one together. This answer is stated right in the words.
Bats sleep all day in dark caves. They hunt for insects at night.
steps.displayText: Bats sleep all day in dark caves. They hunt for insects at night.

steps.ttsScript: When do bats hunt? At night.
How do you know? The words say it right there. That's stated evidence.
You backed your answer with the words. That's how detectives read nonfiction!
Now a tricky one. This answer is hidden. We infer it from clues.
The desert sand burned Maya's feet. She gulped her whole water bottle fast.
steps.displayText: The desert sand burned Maya's feet. She gulped her whole water bottle fast.

steps.ttsScript: Was the desert hot or cold? Hot!
The story never says hot. Burning sand and gulping water are the clues.
You inferred it! The clues led you to a hidden answer. Nice detective work!

slides.heading: The Hidden Clue
slides.imagePrompt: A child kneeling on cracked, sun-baked desert sand under a blazing yellow sun, wiping sweat from their forehead and tipping back an empty water bottle, with rolling sand dunes and shimmering heat lines in the background. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated warm colors, no text.

steps.ttsScript: Here is a handy trick.
If the answer is hidden, ask: what do the clues prove?

Your turn, detective! The story never says it, so use the clues. Listen closely. Ben zipped up his thick coat and pulled his wool hat down low. His breath made little white clouds, and he stomped his boots to warm his toes. What season is it?

interactive.hint: The story never says. What do the clues prove?
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. The story never says the season, so use the clues. A thick coat and breath you can see mean it is very cold. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes, winter! The thick coat, white breath, and cold toes were your clues. You inferred a hidden answer!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Find the stated answers and infer the hidden ones.

steps.ttsScript: Some texts explain how things work or how to do something.
These are called procedural texts, and they help us understand a sequence of steps.
Understanding the order, or sequence, of events is key to following these texts.

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen carefully to these steps. First, you get two slices of bread. Next, you spread peanut butter on one slice. Then, you spread jelly on the other slice. Finally, you put the two slices together. What happens next after you get the bread?

steps.ttsScript: You've got the tools now! The word 'biology' has the root 'bio', which means life. So what does biology study?

interactive.hint: The root 'bio' means life.
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! Remember the root 'bio' gives you the clue. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Exactly! You used the root 'bio' to unlock that it studies living things.

steps.ttsScript: Texts are organized in patterns.
One pattern compares and contrasts ideas.
Another pattern shows a problem and its solution.
A compare and contrast text
shows how things are alike
and how they are different.
A problem and solution text
names a trouble,
then shows how to fix it.
Let's try one together. Listen to this short passage.
Cats and dogs are both pets. But cats purr, while dogs bark.
steps.displayText: Cats and dogs are both pets. But cats purr, while dogs bark.

steps.ttsScript: How are they alike? Both are pets!
What text structure is it? Compare and contrast!
Alike and different words show it's compare and contrast. You spotted the structure!

slides.heading: Let's Try One
slides.imagePrompt: A friendly cat and a friendly dog sitting side by side, the cat curled up purring and the dog mid-bark, shown together to compare and contrast two pets. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors, no text.

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
Words like both, but, and however signal compare and contrast.
Now you are ready to spot text structures!

Your turn, detective! Listen to this little story. A squirrel couldn't reach the nuts on the high branch. So, it found a long stick and used it to knock the nuts down. What text structure did I just use?

interactive.hint: Think about if there was a trouble and how it got fixed.
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Did the story show a trouble that got fixed? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Problem and solution! The squirrel had a trouble, then fixed it with a stick!

steps.ttsScript: A firsthand account is from someone who was there.
A secondhand account is from someone who learned about it.

steps.ttsScript: A diary entry is firsthand.

steps.displayText: In 1849, thousands of miners traveled west to find gold.

steps.ttsScript: Was the writer there? No!
So what kind of account is it? Secondhand!
It gives facts and dates, not feelings, so it is secondhand. You figured it out!

steps.ttsScript: Firsthand has personal feelings and senses. Secondhand has more facts and dates.

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
Ask: was the writer actually there? If yes, firsthand!

slides.heading: Ask: Were They There?

steps.ttsScript: Now you try! Listen carefully. 'The sun was so hot on my face as I walked through the desert. I felt the sand crunch under my boots, and I saw a lizard dart behind a rock!' What kind of account is this?

interactive.hint: Remember, did someone see it happen or learn about it?
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! Think about if the person was *there* to experience it, or if they *learned about* it from someone else. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! That's a Firsthand account because the person was there and shared their own feelings and senses!

steps.ttsScript: Information can come in text, charts, or diagrams.
When you put two forms together, you understand even more.
A chart shows the numbers.
Words explain the why behind those numbers.
Use both, and you get the whole story.
Let's try one together. Here are two sources about a school garden.
steps.displayText: Two sources: a chart and text

steps.ttsScript: The chart says: June, ten tomatoes. July, forty tomatoes.
The text says: In July, the class added water every day.
What does the chart show? More tomatoes in July!
Why did that happen? The text tells us: they watered every day. Combine both and you know the why!
Here is a helpful trick.
Let the chart tell you WHAT. Let the words tell you WHY.
Now you can pull the answer from both forms at once!

Now you try, researcher! A chart shows a town got way more rain in spring than in summer. A text says the town's flowers bloomed most in spring. Using BOTH sources, why did the flowers bloom most in spring?

interactive.hint: Match what the chart shows to what the text says.
interactive.prompt: Why did flowers bloom most in spring?
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite! Look at the chart and the text together. What did BOTH say happened most in spring? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! You combined both sources! The chart showed the most rain in spring, and the text showed the most blooms in spring. That's synthesizing!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn! Let's combine sources to find the full answer.
Authors make a point. Then they back it up with reasons and evidence.
A reason answers one question: why?
It explains why the author's point is true.
Evidence is proof. Facts, examples, and numbers.
Let's try one together. Here is a short paragraph from an author.
Recess is important. Kids think better after they move. Studies show grades rise twenty percent.
steps.displayText: Recess is important. Kids think better after moving. Studies show grades rise twenty percent.

steps.ttsScript: What is the author's point? Recess is important!
What is the reason? Kids think better after they move!
What is the evidence? Grades rise twenty percent! That number is the proof.
Here is a handy trick.
Highlight reasons one color and evidence another. Watch how they link to the point!

slides.heading: Two Colors
slides.imagePrompt: Two highlighter markers (one yellow, one pink) coloring over two short stacked blank text lines on a paper card, the yellow line and pink line connected by a small linking arrow, bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors, blue background, no letters or words.

steps.ttsScript: Now you try, detective! Here's a new paragraph: Dogs make great pets. They keep you active. A vet counted that dog owners walk three miles every day. Which part is the evidence?

interactive.hint: Evidence is proof, like a fact or a number.
interactive.wrongScript: Close! Look for the part with a fact or number that proves the point. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! 'Three miles a day' is a number, so it's the proof that backs up the point!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn to hunt for reasons and evidence on your own!
When we read two texts on the same topic,
we get a stronger understanding.
Take a fact from the first text
and join it with a fact from the second.
Each text may cover a different angle.
Use both to see the whole picture.
Let's try one together. Here are two short texts about owls.
Text one says: Owls hunt at night. Text two says: Owls have huge eyes.
steps.displayText: 1: Owls hunt at night. 2: Owls have huge eyes.

steps.ttsScript: What does text one tell us? Owls hunt at night!
What does text two tell us? Owls have huge eyes!
Put them together: huge eyes help owls hunt in the dark! You combined both texts!

steps.ttsScript: Here's a handy trick for joining two texts.
Ask: how do the facts connect?
The connection is your new, bigger idea.
Now you try! One text says bees make sweet honey. Another text says bees carry pollen to flowers. Put both texts together. What do you understand better about bees?

interactive.hint: Join BOTH facts — what bigger idea do they show?
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Put both facts together — sweet honey AND pollen for flowers. What bigger idea does that show? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Why bees matter! Honey plus pollen shows bees help us and help flowers — you combined both texts!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn to combine two texts!
Find each fact, then join them.
Fourth graders read long nonfiction articles, packed with lots of facts.
A long article can feel like a lot. But every section has one main idea holding it together.
All the other sentences are supporting details. They explain or prove that main idea.
The main idea is the one big point the whole paragraph is about.
Supporting details are the small facts, like numbers or examples, that back it up.
In a long text, read the whole section before you decide the main idea.
Then ask: what one idea do all these details point to?
steps.displayText: Ask: what do details point to?

steps.ttsScript: Let's try one together. Here is a section from a nonfiction article.
Volcanoes can change the land fast. Lava buries forests. Ash covers nearby towns. New rock builds taller mountains.
steps.displayText: Volcanoes change the land fast. Lava buries forests. Ash covers towns. New rock builds mountains.

steps.ttsScript: First, the small details: lava, ash, and new rock. These are the proof.
Now, what one idea do they all point to? Volcanoes change the land.
See? The details all proved one big main idea. You found it!

steps.ttsScript: Here's a trick. The main idea is the glue, and details are what it holds together.
If a sentence is just one small fact, it's a detail, not the main idea.

interactive.wrongScript: That's a detail, not the main idea. Ask: what one idea do all the animals point to? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Coral reefs are full of life! The fish, crabs, and eels were all details that proved it.

steps.ttsScript: Now it's time to practice finding the main idea in longer texts on your own!

steps.ttsScript: Did you know that many big words are made of smaller parts?
A prefix is a word part added to the beginning of a word that changes its meaning.
A suffix is a word part added to the end of a word that changes its meaning or how it's used.
Knowing these parts helps you decode long words and understand what they mean.

steps.ttsScript: Big, exact words make your writing sound smart and clear.
Today we learn three: examine, enormous, and clever.
Examine means to look at something very closely.
Enormous means very, very big.
Clever means smart and quick at solving things.
Plain sentence: The dog is really big.
Better sentence: The dog is enormous!
Let's try one together. Here's a plain sentence to upgrade.
The sentence is: Maria looked at the bug.
Which big word means looked at closely? Examine!
So the upgraded sentence is: Maria examined the bug. Much stronger!
You swapped a plain word for a precise one. That's real vocabulary power!
Here is a helpful trick.
Learn one new word each day. Use it three times!
Soon those words will feel like your own!
Now you try! Match each big word to what it means.

interactive.hint: Picture using each word in a sentence. What would it describe?
interactive.prompt: Match each word to its meaning
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Think about what each word really means, then try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! Examine is look closely, enormous is very big, and clever is smart and quick!

steps.ttsScript: Some words are long! But long words can hide a secret. They split into two little pieces.
Each piece is called a syllable. Two pieces means two syllables.
If we split the word, read each piece, then blend it back, we can read any big word!
Here is the trick. When you see two of the same letter in the middle, split right between them. We call them twin letters.
Look at rabbit. It has twin b's in the middle. Split between them: rab and bit. Rabbit!
Let us try one together. Here is the word: happen.
What are the twin letters in the middle? The two p's!
Split right between the p's. Hap and pen.
Read each piece. Hap. Pen. Now blend them. Happen!
Here is a trick to remember. Find the twin letters first, then split right between them.
Twin letters always tell you where to break the word in two!
Your turn! Match each word to its split. Remember, break right between the twin letters in the middle.

interactive.hint: Find the twin letters, then split between them.
interactive.prompt: Match each word to its split
interactive.wrongScript: Good try! Look for the two same letters in the middle, then split between them. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! Muffin breaks at the twin f's, ladder at the twin d's, and butter at the twin t's!

steps.ttsScript: Most words you can sound out. But a few are sneaky!
We call them sight words. They do not follow the usual sound rules.
So we cannot sound them out. We learn them by sight and remember them.
Look at this word. It is spelled S A I D.
If we sounded it out, we would say sayed. But it really sounds like sed!
There is no rule for this one. We just remember it by sight.
Here is was. It is spelled W A S, but it sounds like wuz.
Here is of. It is spelled O F, but it sounds like uv.
We do not sound these out. We just know them by sight.
Let's try one together. Here is a tricky word.
Here is the word: the.
How is it spelled? T H E!
Can we sound it out? No! We just know it by sight.
So the is a sight word. We remember it. Great job!
Here is a helpful trick. The more you see a sight word, the faster you know it.
Flash cards help! Soon you will read these words in a snap.
Now you try! One of these is a sight word we just remember, not sound out. Is it said, cat, or run?

interactive.hint: Which word can't be sounded out?
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! Cat and run we can sound out. Which one do we just remember? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Said is a sight word. We remember it because we cannot sound it out!

steps.ttsScript: Most verbs add E-D to talk about yesterday. Walk becomes walked!
But some verbs are tricky. They change into a whole new word!
We call these irregular verbs. Let us learn them!
Today I run. But yesterday I did not say runned!
Yesterday I ran. Run changes into ran!
Eat becomes ate. Go becomes went. See becomes saw.
They never add E-D. The whole word switches!
Let us try one together. Here is a sentence about today.
Today the birds fly south.
What is the verb? Fly!
Now make it yesterday. Fly becomes flew!
Yesterday the birds flew south. We did not say flyed!

steps.ttsScript: Here is a trick. Say it out loud with the word yesterday.

interactive.wrongScript: Good try! Say each one with yesterday and listen for what sounds right. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! Run becomes ran, eat becomes ate, and go becomes went. These verbs change the whole word!

steps.ttsScript: You can spot tricky verbs now. Time to practice!
Two tiny marks do big jobs in sentences.
Apostrophes and commas.
Let us learn both!
An apostrophe replaces missing letters in a contraction.
Do not becomes don't. Can not becomes can't.
A comma separates each item when you name three or more things.
I packed socks, shoes, and a hat.
Let's try one together. Here's a sentence with a list.
Sam fed the dog the cat the fish. The marks are missing!
steps.displayText: Sam fed the dog the cat the fish

steps.ttsScript: How many things did Sam feed? Three animals!
What goes between them? A comma after each one!
So we write: Sam fed the dog, the cat, and the fish. Great work!
Here's a trick. A comma is a tiny pause.
Read your list out loud. Every spot you breathe needs a comma.
Now you are ready to use both marks!
Now you try! Here is a list with the marks missing: My garden grows beans corn and peas. Where do the commas belong?

interactive.hint: A comma goes between each thing in the list.
interactive.prompt: Fix the list: beans corn and peas
interactive.wrongScript: So close! Put a comma between each thing in the list. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! A comma after beans and after corn keeps every item clear!

steps.ttsScript: Most verbs become past tense by adding 'ed' to the end, like 'walk' becomes 'walked'.
But some verbs are tricky and change in a different way.
For example, the past tense of 'go' is not 'goed', it's 'went'.
And the past tense of 'eat' is not 'eated', it's 'ate'.

What is the past tense of it? Ran!
So run becomes ran, not runned. You caught that tricky verb!

steps.ttsScript: When a character talks in a story, two little marks show us their exact words.
Quotation marks are the curly marks that wrap around the spoken words.
A comma is a tiny pause mark that helps set off who is speaking.
Put quotation marks around the exact words a person says, and nothing else.
Maya said, open quote, Hi, close quote. The quotation marks hug only the word Hi.
When the speaker comes first, put a comma right after the speaking word, before the quotation marks open.
Dad said, comma, open quote, Run, close quote. The comma goes after said, before the quote.
Let's try one together. Here's a sentence missing its marks.
Tom said Stop.
Who speaks first? Tom! So a comma goes right after said.
What word does Tom say? Stop! So quotation marks wrap around Stop.
Now it reads: Tom said, comma, open quote, Stop, close quote. You fixed it!

steps.ttsScript: Here's a trick. Read it out loud and pause where the speaker stops talking. That pause is your comma.

interactive.wrongScript: Not yet! Mom speaks first, so listen for the pause right after said. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! The comma goes right after said, because Mom speaks first and then we open the quote!

steps.ttsScript: A pronoun is a short word that stands in for a noun, like she, he, it, or they.
The noun the pronoun stands for is called the antecedent.
Today we make the pronoun agree with its antecedent.
One person or thing is singular. Use it, she, or he.
More than one is plural. Use they or them.
The pronoun must match its antecedent in number.
The dogs ate their food. Dogs is plural, so we use their.
Let's try one together. Here's a sentence with a missing pronoun.
The teacher lost blank keys.
What is the antecedent? Teacher! And teacher is just one person.
One person is singular, so we need a singular pronoun.
The teacher lost her keys. Her matches teacher. You made them agree!

She is looking through her open purse with one hand while her other hand pats her hip pocket, displaying a slightly puzzled but pleasant expression. A small bunch of silver keys is partially visible, peeking out from under a colorful stack of papers on the desk, just out of her line of sight. Solid light-yellow background. Bright 2D cartoon illustration, bold clean outlines, vibrant saturated colors. Absolutely no text, no letters, no words, no numbers, no labels anywhere. Natural proportions, no AI artifacts, no eye glints.

steps.ttsScript: Before you pick a pronoun, point back to the noun it replaces.
Ask one or many? One uses she, he, or it. Many uses they.
Your turn! Listen to this sentence: The kittens chased blank tails. Which pronoun makes it agree?

interactive.hint: Kittens is more than one. Which pronoun means many?
interactive.wrongScript: Almost! How many kittens are there? More than one needs their. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Their! Kittens is plural, so the plural pronoun their agrees.

steps.ttsScript: Time to practice! Let's make more pronouns agree with their nouns.
Fourth graders master two big punctuation jobs.
First, commas that join two complete sentences.
Second, quotation marks that wrap what a character says out loud.
When two complete sentences join with and, but, or so, put a comma before that joining word.
Look: I packed my bag, and I caught the bus.
steps.displayText: I packed my bag, and I caught the bus.

steps.ttsScript: Both halves stand alone as sentences, so the comma sits right in front of and.
When a character speaks out loud, wrap their exact words in quotation marks.
Read this: Mia shouted, Wait for me!
A comma comes after shouted, and the end mark tucks inside the closing quote.
Let's try one together. Here is a line where someone speaks.
Here is the line: Sam grinned and whispered, I found the treasure.
steps.displayText: Sam grinned and whispered, “I found the treasure.”

steps.ttsScript: What marks should wrap the words Sam speaks out loud? Quotation marks!
Where does the period belong? Inside! It tucks right inside the closing quote.
So we write: Sam whispered, open-quote I found the treasure period close-quote. You nailed it!
Here is a quick trick for both marks.
Ask: is someone speaking? Use quotes. Are two sentences joined? Use a comma.
Two questions, two marks. Now you are ready!
Your turn! Punctuation has two big jobs. Match each writing situation to the mark it needs.

interactive.hint: Spoken words need quotes; two joined sentences need a comma.
interactive.prompt: Match each situation to its mark
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite. Think about each job: who is speaking, and where do sentences join? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: You matched them all! Spoken words get quotes, joined sentences get a comma, and the period tucks inside.

steps.ttsScript: Good writers choose just the right words to make their stories shine.
Using precise words helps your reader imagine exactly what is happening.
For example, instead of 'walked,' you could say 'tiptoed' if someone was quiet.

These precise words paint a clearer picture and make your writing more exciting!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, detective! Listen closely to this story. Leo loved reading. On Monday, he read a funny story about a talking cat. On Tuesday, he read a book about a rocket going to the moon. He thought the moon book was a little tricky, but he kept reading! Now, what kind of story did Leo read on Monday?

interactive.hint: Think about the first story Leo read.
interactive.wrongScript: Not quite! Listen to the story again and think about the very first book Leo read. Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Exactly! Leo read a funny story on Monday. It's great to mix up the kinds of books you read!

steps.ttsScript: Fact books are full of real things from our world.
They can be about anything! Animals, space, weather, food.
Let us learn how to read fact books all year.

steps.ttsScript: Pick fact books about things you love.
Love bugs? Read about insects. Love space? Read about planets.
You will have so much more fun when it is your favorite topic.

steps.ttsScript: Fact books have helpers built in.
Headings. Pictures. Labels. Use them to find facts fast.
They are like a roadmap through the book!

steps.ttsScript: Okay, let's try one together! Here's a page from a fact book about bats.
Bats sleep all day. They fly at night to catch moths.
steps.displayText: Bats sleep all day. They fly at night to catch moths.

steps.ttsScript: When do bats sleep? All day!
steps.displayText: When do bats sleep? All day!

steps.ttsScript: When do they fly? At night!
steps.displayText: When do they fly? At night!

steps.ttsScript: You found real facts about bats! That's how we read a fact book. Great reading!

steps.ttsScript: Here is a helpful trick.
Try a new topic each week. You will discover something you did not know!
Now you are ready to explore fact books all year!

steps.ttsScript: Your turn, fact finder! Ben wanted the page about frogs. He did not look at any pictures. He read the bold titles at the top of each page until he found the frog page. What helped Ben find the right page?

interactive.hint: She read the bold titles at the top of each page.
interactive.wrongScript: Good try! Think back, Ben read the bold titles at the top of each page. Which part was that? Try again!
interactive.correctScript: Yes! Headings are the titles that name each page, so they led Ben right to frogs!
